title: The Republic
author: Plato
summary: The Republic is Plato’s extended dialogue where Socrates and interlocutors examine justice, constructing an ideal city to define the just soul. Through debate with Cephalus, Polemarchus, Thrasymachus, Glaucon, and Adeimantus, Socrates outlines guardian classes, education in music and gymnastic, censorship of poets, communal property and families for rulers, and the philosopher‑king who grasps the Form of the Good. The work traces degeneration from aristocracy to other regimes, analyses individual psychology, defends philosophical rule, and culminates in criticisms of poetry and an argument for the soul’s immortality and moral reward, and offers an education for lifelong philosophy study.

characters.name: Socrates
characters.personality: inquisitive, ironic, dialectical; leads enquiry into justice
characters.lifespan: Conducts the dialogues throughout the work; survives as principal interlocutor and organizer of the philosophical construction

characters.name: Cephalus
characters.personality: just, blameless, conservative elder who opens the discussion
characters.lifespan: Appears early to introduce the question of justice and then withdraws from the main argument

characters.name: Polemarchus
characters.personality: defends conventional morality and familial obligations
characters.lifespan: Takes over from Cephalus and participates in early exchanges; remains an interlocutor

characters.name: Thrasymachus
characters.personality: aggressive, sophistical, cynical about justice as advantage of the stronger
characters.lifespan: Challenges Socrates, is argued against and partially refuted; exits the scene still contentious

characters.name: Glaucon
characters.personality: ambitious, provocative, tests and amplifies sceptical positions
characters.lifespan: Demands fuller account of justice; helps drive construction of the ideal State; remains active in dialogue

characters.name: Adeimantus
characters.personality: reflective, critical, supports Glaucon's challenge and highlights public perception
characters.lifespan: Participates in the extended argument and helps frame deeper objections; remains present at conclusion
